message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-009: run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run (success and failure paths)
File: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:351
Description: run_study records a hash-chained provenance trail and stores experiment_run_id inside the run_end entry (pipeline.py:721), but never writes the trail anywhere durable nor attaches it to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker and run_id are known. Persistence is opt-in (StudyResult.persist_trail() at :226 + a manual tracker.log_artifact). So the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but no link back to the append-only trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. The failure path is worse: the run_failed entry (pipeline.py:732) omits experiment_run_id even though run_id is in scope and the finally block terminates the MLflow run as FAILED (:754) — the case where correlation matters most leaves a FAILED run and an in-memory-only trail sharing no recorded id. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim'.
PoC: run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()): on success the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json; StudyResult.provenance is gone on exit. With a discover() that raises, the run is set FAILED but run_failed has no experiment_run_id — given the FAILED run id you cannot find its trail, and vice versa.
Suggested fix: When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study persist the trail to a working path and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) on the success path (best-effort partial trail on failure), guarded like the other track() calls. Add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed trail.record() payload (deterministic, preserves hash-determinism) so failed runs are equally correlatable across the trail and the experiment store.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

706	            # the common case but a real cost for very large trails, so a perf-sensitive caller
707	            # that trusts the in-process trail may pass verify_trail=False (pass 3, #F-036). The
708	            # important re-verification — of a trail deserialized from an external store — is the

711	        # Terminal success entry (pass 3, #F-006): a phase 'report' record is not a run-level
712	        # terminus, so a trail truncated mid-run is otherwise indistinguishable from one that
713	        # completed. Record run_end ONLY after verify() passes, so its presence as the last
714	        # entry is an affirmative "this run finished with an intact chain" marker. The payload is
715	        # deterministic (no wall-clock) so the provenance-hash determinism guarantee holds — the
716	        # non-deterministic run duration goes to the experiment tracker as an SLO metric instead.
717	        trail.record(

content.text:
Confirmed. The body of `run_study` contains no `persist_trail` or `log_artifact` call. The trail is only attached to `StudyResult.provenance` (in-memory tuple), and persistence is entirely opt-in via the caller calling `StudyResult.persist_trail()` then `tracker.log_artifact()` manually.

Let me verify the two specific PoC claims precisely:

1. Success path: MLflow run gets params/metrics but no artifact, and trail evaporates — confirmed (no log_artifact anywhere).
2. Failure path: `run_failed` (line 731-737) omits `experiment_run_id` — confirmed; the payload only has `error_type`, `message`, `last_phase`. Meanwhile `run_end` (line 717-723) DOES include `experiment_run_id=run_id`. And the finally block sets the run FAILED (line 754).

This is an asymmetry confirmed in code: success path records `experiment_run_id` in the trail (but still doesn't persist trail as artifact), failure path does not record it at all.

Let me check the GOVERNANCE reference to validate the "spine of reproducibility" claim weight.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rn "spine of reproducibility\|independence claim\|append-only trail" GOVERNANCE* docs/ *.md 2>/dev/null | head
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rln "spine of reproducibility\|independence claim" . --include=*.md --include=*.rst --include=*.txt 2>/dev/null | head
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rl "spine of reproducibility" . 2>/dev/null | head; echo "---"; ls docs/ 2>/dev/null; find . -iname "*govern*" 2>/dev/null | head

content.text: Let me read the governance section more fully to understand whether persistence-to-tracker is in scope or explicitly deferred.

content.content:
85	## 3. Provenance
86	
87	An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
88	executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
89	reproducibility and of the independence claim.
90	
91	> **Implementation status (engine):** wired (ADR-0010). `assay_engine.provenance.ProvenanceTrail`
92	> is an append-only, hash-chained trail; the study runner (`assay_engine.pipeline.run_study`)
93	> records every action — ingest, blind baseline, each locked hypothesis (discovery *and*
94	> claim-derived), every gate decision, each verdict, the scorecard, the report — *before the next
95	> runs*, and verifies the chain before returning. There is no remove/edit/reorder API.
96	> **Integrity, honestly, in two tiers:** the default unkeyed chain is *tamper-evident against
97	> naive tampering and accidental corruption* (a single-entry edit, a reorder, or a deletion is
98	> caught by `verify_records`) but is **not** forgery-proof — a party who controls the serialized
99	> bytes can edit a payload and recompute the whole SHA-256 chain. Pass a `secret=` and the chain
100	> is HMAC-keyed, so the head cannot be recomputed without the secret (a downstream store cannot
101	> silently rewrite and re-seal history); `verify_records` must be given the same secret. An
102	> in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process, and non-repudiable third-party
103	> attestation of the trail's *time* is the same pluggable concern as pre-registration (§2),
104	> out of scope here. A caller may pass its own trail to `run_study` so a run that *raises* (a
105	> blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail.
106	>
107	> The synchronous `run_study` path records all of the above into the chained trail by default.
108	> The **durable LangGraph gate path** (`make_gate_node`) records decisions into LangGraph
109	> `gate_decisions` *state*, which is not the hash-chained trail; to bring that path under §3
110	> integrity, wire `make_gate_node(..., recorder=...)` to a `ProvenanceTrail` (the recorder
111	> bridge, #111). Without that bridge, the graph path's gate decisions are graph state, not
112	> tamper-evident trail entries.
113	
114	## 4. Single-operator structural independence
115	
116	Independence from operator bias is achieved structurally, not by a co-reviewer:
117	
118	- **Code gates** the operator cannot silently bypass.
119	- **Append-only provenance** that records the full decision history.
120	- **Timestamped pre-registration** committing to hypotheses before they are tested.
121	- **A complete reproducibility package** published with findings.
122	
123	This is a deliberate methodological position and is stated explicitly in any output.
124

content.text:
This is the key nuance. The governance doc explicitly describes the trail as in-memory and addresses durability:

- Line 102-104: "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process... A caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that raises... still leaves an auditable partial trail."
- The documented design pattern (StudyResult.persist_trail docstring) is that the **caller** persists the trail and attaches it via log_artifact.

So the in-memory nature is intended design, and persistence is explicitly delegated to the caller. The finding's framing that "the trail evaporates on process exit" by default is true but is documented intended behavior, not a defect — the caller owns durability.

However, let me assess the more concrete sub-claim: the **asymmetry** between `run_end` (records `experiment_run_id`) and `run_failed` (does not). This is a genuine, narrow inconsistency in the code regardless of the persistence design philosophy. The success terminus correlates the trail to the experiment store via the embedded run_id; the failure terminus does not, even though `run_id` is in scope and the finally block marks the MLflow run FAILED. That breaks correlation precisely on the failure path.

Let me confirm `run_id` is in scope at line 731 and is unconditionally available.

content.content:
309	    trail = trail if trail is not None else ProvenanceTrail(secret=secret, clock=clock)
310	    visited: list[Phase] = []
311	    run_id: str | None = None
312	
313	    def enter(phase: Phase, **payload: Any) -> None:
314	        visited.append(phase)
315	        trail.record("phase", f"enter {phase.name}", phase=phase.name, **payload)
316	
317	    def track(fn: Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]) -> None:
318	        # observability is best-effort: a tracker failure is recorded but never aborts a run.
319	        if tracker is None or run_id is None:
320	            return
321	        try:
322	            fn(tracker, run_id)

content.text:
Confirmed: `run_id` is in scope at line 731 (initialized at 311, set at 353). It is `None` when no tracker/start failed, else the MLflow run id. So `experiment_run_id=run_id` could be added to the `run_failed` payload deterministically (same as `run_end` already does), and it would correlate the FAILED MLflow run to its trail.

Now let me weigh the overall finding. Two distinct claims:

**Claim A (persistence/artifact attachment):** run_study never persists the trail nor calls log_artifact even when tracker+run_id known. **TRUE in code, but documented intended design** — GOVERNANCE §3 and the persist_trail docstring explicitly state the trail is in-memory and the caller owns durability/artifact attachment. So "no artifacts, no provenance.json by default" is by-design, not a defect. This part is closer to a design-enhancement suggestion than a production-readiness bug.

**Claim B (failure-path correlation asymmetry):** `run_failed` omits `experiment_run_id` while `run_end` includes it; the finally block marks the MLflow run FAILED. **TRUE in code, and a genuine inconsistency.** This is a real, concrete, low-effort gap: on the failure path — exactly where post-mortem correlation matters most — there is no recorded link between the FAILED MLflow run and the trail, even though `run_end` establishes that link on success. This is a real defect (asymmetry), not speculative.

The finding bundles both. Claim A's "evaporates / no durable persistence" is intended design and not a true production-readiness problem on its own. Claim B is a real, reproducible inconsistency but is minor (the correlation link still exists in one direction conceptually via the StudyResult.experiment_run_id, and the trail is caller-owned). 

Let me verify whether the StudyResult is even returned on the failure path (it re-raises), which determines whether ANY correlation survives a failure.

The `except` block at line 726 re-raises (line 748), so on failure **no StudyResult is returned** — the only surviving artifact is the caller-owned trail (if the caller passed one in). That trail's `run_failed` entry indeed lacks `experiment_run_id`. So Claim B is materially correct: a caller holding the FAILED MLflow run id and the partial trail has no recorded id linking them.

I'll confirm the finding as a real but **low-severity** production-readiness concern. The core durability framing (Claim A) is intended design and somewhat overstated, but the concrete failure-path correlation asymmetry (Claim B) is a genuine, reproducible, fixable defect that the description and suggested fix correctly identify.

input.reasoning:
Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py.

CONFIRMED facts in code:
1. run_study never calls persist_trail() or tracker.log_artifact() in its body. grep across pipeline.py shows persist_trail/log_artifact appear ONLY in StudyResult.persist_trail (line 226) and its docstring (line 232); the run_study body has none. The trail lives only as the in-memory tuple StudyResult.provenance (line 765). So a default governed run (tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker(), auto-approve gate) yields an MLflow run with params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json. The MlflowExperimentTracker DOES expose log_artifact (observability/tracking.py:92-93), so attachment is technically available but never invoked.
2. The success terminus run_end (lines 717-723) records experiment_run_id=run_id, deterministically linking the trail to the MLflow run.
3. The failure terminus run_failed (lines 731-737) records ONLY error_type/message/last_phase and OMITS experiment_run_id, even though run_id is in scope (declared :311, set :353) and the finally block marks the MLflow run FAILED (line 754). On failure the except block re-raises (line 748), so NO StudyResult is returned — the only surviving artifact is the caller-owned partial trail, whose run_failed entry has no recorded link to the FAILED MLflow run id. This is a genuine, reproducible asymmetry exactly as the PoC describes.

NUANCE / why LOW not higher:
- The "trail evaporates / no durable persistence by default" framing (Claim A) is INTENDED, documented design, not a defect. GOVERNANCE.md §3 (lines 102-105) explicitly states "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process" and that a caller may pass its own trail so a raising run leaves an auditable partial trail; the persist_trail docstring (lines 229-233) explicitly delegates durability and log_artifact attachment to the caller. So default in-memory behavior and caller-owned persistence is by-design, and that portion of the finding is an enhancement suggestion rather than a production-readiness bug.
- The concrete, real defect is the failure-path correlation asymmetry (Claim B): run_end embeds experiment_run_id but run_failed does not, despite run_id being available and the run being marked FAILED. This is a true, deterministic, trivially-fixable inconsistency (add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed payload, preserving hash-determinism just as run_end does) and the suggested fix is correct.

Net: the finding is confirmed (the described code behavior is real and the PoC reproduces), but its severity is low — the durability portion is intended design, and the only genuine bug (failure-path run_id omission) is a minor correlation gap with no security/data-loss/availability impact, affecting auditability of failed runs only.

content.text:
Verified finding G-009. Confirmed=true, severity=low.

Key evidence at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:
- run_study body never calls persist_trail() or tracker.log_artifact() (grep confirms both names appear only in StudyResult.persist_trail at line 226 and its docstring).
- run_end (lines 717-723) records experiment_run_id=run_id; run_failed (lines 731-737) omits it despite run_id being in scope (declared line 311, set line 353) and the finally block marking the MLflow run FAILED (line 754).

The persistence/durability portion of the finding is intended, documented design (docs/GOVERNANCE.md:102-105 and the persist_trail docstring delegate durability to the caller), so that part is an enhancement rather than a defect. The genuine, reproducible bug is the failure-path correlation asymmetry — fixable by adding experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed payload as the suggested fix states. Hence confirmed but low severity (auditability of failed runs only; no security/data-loss/availability impact).
